([Hindi](/wiki/Hindi_language "Hindi language"): आधुनिक मानक हिन्दी, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Hindi "Romanization of Hindi"): *Ādhunik Mānak Hindī*),[[9]](#cite_note-10) commonly referred to as **Hindi**, is an [Indo-Aryan language](/wiki/Indo-Aryan_languages "Indo-Aryan languages") written in the [Devanagari script](/wiki/Devanagari_script "Devanagari script"). It is an [official language](/wiki/Official_language_of_India "Official language of India") of the [Government of India](/wiki/Government_of_India "Government of India"), and is the *[lingua franca](/wiki/Lingua_franca "Lingua franca")* for most of the northern half of India.[[10]](#cite_note-11)

**Modern Standard Hindi** ([Hindi](/wiki/Hindi_language "Hindi language"): आधुनिक मानक हिन्दी, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Hindi "Romanization of Hindi"): *Ādhunik Mānak Hindī*),[[9]](#cite_note-10) commonly referred to as **Hindi**, is an [Indo-Aryan language](/wiki/Indo-Aryan_languages "Indo-Aryan languages") written in the [Devanagari script](/wiki/Devanagari_script "Devanagari script"). It is an [official language](/wiki/Official_language_of_India "Official language of India") of the [Government of India](/wiki/Government_of_India "Government of India"), and is the *[lingua franca](/wiki/Lingua_franca "Lingua franca")* for most of the northern half of India.[[10]](#cite_note-11)

Hindi is an [official language](/wiki/Languages_with_official_status_in_India "Languages with official status in India") in ten states ([Bihar](/wiki/Bihar "Bihar"), [Chhattisgarh](/wiki/Chhattisgarh "Chhattisgarh"), [Gujarat](/wiki/Gujarat "Gujarat"), [Haryana](/wiki/Haryana "Haryana"), [Himachal Pradesh](/wiki/Himachal_Pradesh "Himachal Pradesh"), [Jharkhand](/wiki/Jharkhand "Jharkhand"), [Madhya Pradesh](/wiki/Madhya_Pradesh "Madhya Pradesh"), [Rajasthan](/wiki/Rajasthan "Rajasthan"), [Uttar Pradesh](/wiki/Uttar_Pradesh "Uttar Pradesh"), [Uttarakhand](/wiki/Uttarakhand "Uttarakhand")), and six [union territories](/wiki/Union_territory "Union territory") ([Andaman and Nicobar Islands](/wiki/Andaman_and_Nicobar_Islands "Andaman and Nicobar Islands"), [Delhi](/wiki/Delhi "Delhi"), [Chandigarh](/wiki/Chandigarh "Chandigarh"), [Dadra and Nagar Haveli and Daman and Diu](/wiki/Dadra_and_Nagar_Haveli_and_Daman_and_Diu "Dadra and Nagar Haveli and Daman and Diu"), [Ladakh](/wiki/Ladakh "Ladakh") and [Jammu and Kashmir](/wiki/Jammu_and_Kashmir_(union_territory) "Jammu and Kashmir (union territory)")) and an additional official language in the states of [West Bengal](/wiki/West_Bengal "West Bengal") and [Maharashtra](/wiki/Maharashtra "Maharashtra"). [[11]](#cite_note-12)[[12]](#cite_note-13)[[13]](#cite_note-:2-14) Hindi is also one of the 22 [scheduled languages of the Republic of India](/wiki/Languages_with_official_status_in_India "Languages with official status in India").[[14]](#cite_note-15)

Apart from the script and formal vocabulary, Modern Standard Hindi is [mutually intelligible](/wiki/Mutual_intelligibility "Mutual intelligibility") with standard [Urdu](/wiki/Urdu "Urdu").[[15]](#cite_note-GubeGao2019-16)[[16]](#cite_note-ChatterjiSiṃhaPadikkal1997-17)[[17]](#cite_note-18)[[18]](#cite_note-Ahmed2024-19) The common base of the two languages is sometimes referred to as *Hindi–Urdu* or [Hindustani](/wiki/Hindustani_language "Hindustani language"), and Hindi is considered a [Sanskritised](/wiki/Sanskritisation_(linguistics) "Sanskritisation (linguistics)") [register](/wiki/Register_(sociolinguistics) "Register (sociolinguistics)")[[19]](#cite_note-Constitution_of_India-20) of Hindustani.[[20]](#cite_note-NCSU-Hindustani-21) Hindi is also spoken, to a lesser extent, in other parts of India (usually in a simplified or pidginised variety such as Bazaar [Hindustani](/wiki/Hindustani_language "Hindustani language") or [Haflong Hindi](/wiki/Haflong_Hindi "Haflong Hindi")).[[21]](#cite_note-fulllangdatacensus_2011-22)[[22]](#cite_note-:0-23) Outside India, several other languages are recognised officially as "Hindi" but do not refer to the Standard Hindi language described here and instead descend from other nearby languages, such as [Awadhi](/wiki/Awadhi_language "Awadhi language") and [Bhojpuri](/wiki/Bhojpuri_language "Bhojpuri language").[[23]](#cite_note-24) Examples of this are Bhojpuri-Hindustani, which is spoken in [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa") and [Mauritius](/wiki/Mauritius "Mauritius"); [Fiji Hindi](/wiki/Fiji_Hindi "Fiji Hindi"), spoken in Fiji; and [Caribbean Hindustani](/wiki/Caribbean_Hindustani "Caribbean Hindustani"), spoken in [Suriname](/wiki/Suriname "Suriname"), [Trinidad and Tobago](/wiki/Trinidad_and_Tobago "Trinidad and Tobago"), and [Guyana](/wiki/Guyana "Guyana").[[24]](#cite_note-auto-25)[[25]](#cite_note-26)[[26]](#cite_note-uniw-27)[[27]](#cite_note-Mau-28)

Hindi is the [fourth-most-spoken first language](/wiki/List_of_languages_by_number_of_native_speakers "List of languages by number of native speakers") in the world, after [Mandarin](/wiki/Mandarin_Chinese "Mandarin Chinese"), [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"), and [English](/wiki/English_language "English language").[[28]](#cite_note-Nationalencyklopedin-29) When counted together with the mutually intelligible Urdu, it is the third most-spoken language in the world, after Mandarin and English.[[29]](#cite_note-Gambhir1995-30)[[30]](#cite_note-31) According to reports of *Ethnologue* (2025), Hindi is the [third most-spoken language](/wiki/List_of_languages_by_total_number_of_speakers#Ethnologue_(2022,_25th_edition) "List of languages by total number of speakers") in the world when including [first](/wiki/First_language "First language") and [second language](/wiki/Second_language "Second language") speakers.[[31]](#cite_note-32)

Hindi is the fastest-growing [language of India](/wiki/Languages_of_India "Languages of India"), followed by [Kashmiri](/wiki/Kashmiri_language "Kashmiri language"), [Meitei](/wiki/Meitei_language "Meitei language"), [Gujarati](/wiki/Gujarati_language "Gujarati language") and [Bengali](/wiki/Bengali_language "Bengali language"), according to the [2011 census of India](/wiki/2011_census_of_India "2011 census of India").[[32]](#cite_note-33)

## Terminology

The term *Hindī* was originally used to refer to inhabitants of the [Indo-Gangetic Plain](/wiki/Indo-Gangetic_Plain "Indo-Gangetic Plain"). It was borrowed from [Classical Persian](/wiki/Persian_language#Classical_Persian "Persian language") هندی *Hindī* ([Iranian Persian](/wiki/Iranian_Persian "Iranian Persian") pronunciation: *Hendi*), meaning 'of or belonging to *[Hind](/wiki/Hindustan "Hindustan")* (India)' (hence, 'Indian').[[33]](#cite_note-34)

Another name *Hindavī* (ہندوی/हिन्दवी) (from [Persian](/wiki/Persian_language "Persian language")  *هندوی* 'of or belonging to the Indian people') was often used in the past, for example, by [Amir Khusrau](/wiki/Amir_Khusrau "Amir Khusrau") in his poetry.[[34]](#cite_note-35)[[35]](#cite_note-36)

The terms *Hindi* and *Hindu* trace back to Old Persian, which derived these names from the [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit") name *Sindhu* (सिन्धु), referring to the [Indus River](/wiki/Indus_River "Indus River"). The Greek cognates of the same terms are *Indus* (for the river) and *India* (for the land of the river).[[36]](#cite_note-Bose2006-37)[[37]](#cite_note-38)

The term *Modern Standard Hindi* is commonly used to specifically refer to the modern literary Hindi language, as opposed to colloquial and regional varieties that are also referred to as [*Hindi* in a wider sense](/wiki/Hindi_belt "Hindi belt").[[38]](#cite_note-kachru-39)

## History

Further information: [History of Hindustani](/wiki/History_of_Hindustani "History of Hindustani")

### Middle Indo-Aryan to Hindi

Like other Indo-Aryan languages, Hindi is a direct descendant of an early form of [Vedic Sanskrit](/wiki/Vedic_Sanskrit "Vedic Sanskrit") (not to be confused with the later variety of [Classical Sanskrit](/wiki/Classical_Sanskrit "Classical Sanskrit")). This early variety transitioned in [medieval India](/wiki/Medieval_India "Medieval India") into [Shauraseni Prakrit](/wiki/Shauraseni_Prakrit "Shauraseni Prakrit") and [Śauraseni Apabhraṃśa](/wiki/Apabhra%E1%B9%83%C5%9Ba "Apabhraṃśa") (from Sanskrit *apabhraṃśa* "corrupt"), which emerged in the 7th century CE.[[39]](#cite_note-de-40)

The sound changes that characterised the transition from Middle Indo-Aryan to Hindi are:[[40]](#cite_note-41)

* [Compensatory lengthening](/wiki/Compensatory_lengthening "Compensatory lengthening") of vowels preceding [geminate](/wiki/Geminate "Geminate") consonants, sometimes with spontaneous nasalisation: Skt. *hasta* "hand" > Pkt. *hattha* > *hāth*
* Loss of all word-final vowels: *rātri* "night" > *rattī* > *rāt*
* Formation of nasalised long vowels from nasal consonants (-VNC- > -V̄̃C-): *bandha* "bond" > *bā̃dh*
* Loss of unaccented or unstressed short vowels (reflected in [schwa deletion](/wiki/Schwa_deletion "Schwa deletion")): *susthira* "firm" > *sutthira* > *suthrā*
* Collapsing of adjacent vowels (including separated by a hiatus: *apara* "other" > *avara* > *aur*
* Final *-m* to *-ṽ*: *grāma* "village" > *gāma* > *gāṽ*
* Intervocalic *-ḍ-* to *-ṛ-* or *-l-*: *taḍāga* "pond" > *talāv*, *naḍa* "reed" > *nal*.
* *v* > *b*: *vivāha* "marriage" > *byāh*

### Hindustani

During the period of [Delhi Sultanate](/wiki/Delhi_Sultanate "Delhi Sultanate") in [medieval India](/wiki/Medieval_India "Medieval India"), which covered most of today's north India, eastern Pakistan, southern Nepal and Bangladesh[[41]](#cite_note-42) and which resulted in the [contact of Hindu and Muslim cultures](/wiki/Ganga-Jamuni_tehzeeb "Ganga-Jamuni tehzeeb"), the [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit") and [Prakrit](/wiki/Prakrit "Prakrit") base of [Old Hindi](/wiki/Old_Hindi "Old Hindi") became enriched with loanwords from [Persian](/wiki/Persian_language "Persian language"), evolving into the present form of Hindustani.[[42]](#cite_note-Rekhta2020-43)[[43]](#cite_note-MatthewsShackleHusain1985-44)[[44]](#cite_note-Dhulipala2000-45)[[45]](#cite_note-IJSW1943-46)[[46]](#cite_note-Mody2008-47)[[47]](#cite_note-Kesavan1997-48) Hindi achieved prominence in India after it became the official language of the [imperial court](/wiki/Government_of_the_Mughal_Empire "Government of the Mughal Empire") during the reign of [Shah Jahan](/wiki/Shah_Jahan "Shah Jahan").[[48]](#cite_note-49) It is recorded that Emperor [Aurangzeb](/wiki/Aurangzeb "Aurangzeb") spoke in [Hindvi](/wiki/Hindustani_language "Hindustani language").[[49]](#cite_note-50) The Hindustani vernacular became an expression of Indian national unity during the [Indian Independence movement](/wiki/Indian_independence_movement "Indian independence movement"),[[50]](#cite_note-Hock1991-51)[[51]](#cite_note-:1-52) and continues to be spoken as the common language of the people of the northern Indian subcontinent,[[52]](#cite_note-Ashmore1961-53) which is reflected in the [Hindustani vocabulary](/wiki/Hindustani_vocabulary "Hindustani vocabulary") of [Bollywood](/wiki/Bollywood "Bollywood") films and songs.[[53]](#cite_note-Tunstall2008-54)[[54]](#cite_note-Hiro2015-55)

Standard Hindi is based on the [Khariboli](/wiki/Kauravi_dialect "Kauravi dialect") dialect, spoken in the [Ganges-Yamuna Doab](/wiki/Western_Uttar_Pradesh "Western Uttar Pradesh") ([Delhi](/wiki/Delhi "Delhi"), [Meerut](/wiki/Meerut "Meerut"), and [Saharanpur](/wiki/Saharanpur "Saharanpur"));[[39]](#cite_note-de-40)[[55]](#cite_note-Britannica2000-56) the vernacular of [Delhi](/wiki/Delhi "Delhi") and the surrounding region gradually replaced earlier prestige languages such as [Awadhi](/wiki/Awadhi_language "Awadhi language") and [Braj](/wiki/Braj "Braj"). Standard Hindi was developed by supplanting foreign loanwords from the Hindustani language and replacing them with [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit") words, though Standard Hindi does continue to possess several Persian loanwords.[[56]](#cite_note-JainCardona2007-57)[[57]](#cite_note-58)[[58]](#cite_note-Gumperz1971-59) Modern Hindi became a literary language in the 19th century. Earliest examples could be found as *Prēm Sāgar* by [Lallu Lal](/wiki/Lallu_Lal "Lallu Lal"), *Batiyāl Pachīsī* of Sadal Misra, and *Rānī Kētakī Kī Kahānī* of [Insha Allah Khan](/wiki/Insha_Allah_Khan "Insha Allah Khan") which were published in [Devanagari script](/wiki/Devanagari "Devanagari") during the early 19th century.[[59]](#cite_note-60)

[John Gilchrist](/wiki/John_Gilchrist_(linguist) "John Gilchrist (linguist)") was principally known for his study of the [Hindustani language](/wiki/Hindustani_language "Hindustani language"), which was adopted as the [lingua franca](/wiki/Lingua_franca "Lingua franca") of northern India (including what is now present-day [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan")) by Britons and Indians alike. He compiled and authored *An English-Hindustani Dictionary*, *A Grammar of the Hindoostanee Language*, *The Oriental Linguist*, and many more. His [lexicon](/wiki/Lexicon "Lexicon") of Hindustani was published in the [Perso-Arabic script](/wiki/Perso-Arabic_script "Perso-Arabic script"), [Nāgarī script](/wiki/N%C4%81gar%C4%AB_script "Nāgarī script"), and in [Roman transliteration](/wiki/Romanisation "Romanisation"). In the late 19th century, a movement to further develop Hindi as a standardised form of Hindustani separate from Urdu took form. In 1881, [Bihar](/wiki/Bihar "Bihar") accepted Hindi as its sole official language, replacing Urdu, and thus became the first state of India to adopt Hindi.[[60]](#cite_note-61) However, in 2014, Urdu was accorded second official language status in the state.[[61]](#cite_note-62)

### Independent India

On 14 September 1949, the [Constituent Assembly of India](/wiki/Constituent_Assembly_of_India "Constituent Assembly of India") adopted Hindi written in the Devanagari script as the [official language of the Republic of India](/wiki/Official_languages_of_Union_govt_of_India "Official languages of Union govt of India") replacing the previous usage of Hindustani in the Perso-Arabic script in the [British Indian Empire](/wiki/British_Indian_Empire "British Indian Empire").[[62]](#cite_note-63)[[63]](#cite_note-64)[[64]](#cite_note-65) To this end, several stalwarts rallied and lobbied pan-India in favour of Hindi, most notably [Beohar Rajendra Simha](https://hi.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E0%A4%B5%E0%A5%8D%E0%A4%AF%E0%A5%8C%E0%A4%B9%E0%A4%BE%E0%A4%B0_%E0%A4%B0%E0%A4%BE%E0%A4%9C%E0%A5%87%E0%A4%A8%E0%A5%8D%E0%A4%A6%E0%A5%8D%E0%A4%B0_%E0%A4%B8%E0%A4%BF%E0%A4%82%E0%A4%B9 "hi:व्यौहार राजेन्द्र सिंह") along with [Hazari Prasad Dwivedi](/wiki/Hazari_Prasad_Dwivedi "Hazari Prasad Dwivedi"), [Kaka Kalelkar](/wiki/Kaka_Kalelkar "Kaka Kalelkar"), [Maithili Sharan Gupt](/wiki/Maithili_Sharan_Gupt "Maithili Sharan Gupt") and [Seth Govind Das](/wiki/Seth_Govind_Das "Seth Govind Das") who even debated in Parliament on this issue. As such, on the 50th birthday of Beohar Rajendra Simha on 14 September 1949, the efforts came to fruition following the adoption of Hindi as the official language.[[65]](#cite_note-66) Now, it is celebrated as [Hindi Day](/wiki/Hindi_Day "Hindi Day").[[66]](#cite_note-67)

## Official status

### India

Part XVII of the Indian Constitution deals with the official language of the Indian Union. Under Article 343, the official languages of the Union have been prescribed, which includes Hindi in Devanagari script and English:

> (1) The official language of the Union shall be Hindi in Devanagari script. The form of numerals to be used for the official purposes of the Union shall be the international form of Indian numerals.[[24]](#cite_note-auto-25)  
> (2) Notwithstanding anything in clause (1), for a period of fifteen years from the commencement of this Constitution, the English language shall continue to be used for all the official purposes of the Union for which it was being used immediately before such commencement: Provided that the President may, during the said period, by order authorise the use of the Hindi language in addition to the English language and of the Devanagari form of numerals in addition to the international form of Indian numerals for any of the official purposes of the Union.[[67]](#cite_note-NIC2008-68)

It was envisioned that Hindi would become the sole working language of the Union Government by 1965 (per directives in Article 344 (2) and Article 351),[[68]](#cite_note-eighthschedule-69) with state governments being free to function in the language of their own choice. However, widespread resistance to the [imposition of Hindi](/wiki/Imposition_of_Hindi "Imposition of Hindi") on non-native speakers, especially in [South India](/wiki/South_India "South India") (such as [those in Tamil Nadu](/wiki/Anti-Hindi_agitations_of_Tamil_Nadu "Anti-Hindi agitations of Tamil Nadu")) led to the passage of the Official Languages Act of 1963, which provided for the continued use of English indefinitely for all official purposes, although the constitutional directive for the Union Government to encourage the spread of Hindi was retained and has strongly influenced its policies.[[69]](#cite_note-70)

[Article 344 (2b)](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Constitution_of_India/Part_XVII "s:Constitution of India/Part XVII") stipulates that the official language commission shall be constituted every ten years to recommend steps for the progressive use of Hindi language and impose restrictions on the use of the English language by the union government. In practice, the official language commissions are constantly endeavouring to promote Hindi but not imposing restrictions on English in official use by the union government.

At the state level, Hindi is the official language of the following Indian states: [Bihar](/wiki/Bihar "Bihar"), [Chhattisgarh](/wiki/Chhattisgarh "Chhattisgarh"), [Haryana](/wiki/Haryana "Haryana"), [Himachal Pradesh](/wiki/Himachal_Pradesh "Himachal Pradesh"), [Jharkhand](/wiki/Jharkhand "Jharkhand"), [Madhya Pradesh](/wiki/Madhya_Pradesh "Madhya Pradesh"), [Rajasthan](/wiki/Rajasthan "Rajasthan"), [Uttar Pradesh](/wiki/Uttar_Pradesh "Uttar Pradesh") and [Uttarakhand](/wiki/Uttarakhand "Uttarakhand").[[70]](#cite_note-langoff-71) Hindi is an official language of [Gujarat](/wiki/Gujarat "Gujarat"), along with [Gujarati](/wiki/Gujarati_language "Gujarati language").[[71]](#cite_note-Benedikter2009-72) It acts as an additional official language of [West Bengal](/wiki/West_Bengal "West Bengal") in blocks and sub-divisions with more than 10% of the population speaking Hindi.[[72]](#cite_note-73)[[73]](#cite_note-Indiatoday:1-74)[[74]](#cite_note-75) Similarly, Hindi is accorded the status of official language in the following [Union Territories](/wiki/Union_Territories "Union Territories"): [Delhi](/wiki/Delhi "Delhi"), [Andaman and Nicobar Islands](/wiki/Andaman_and_Nicobar_Islands "Andaman and Nicobar Islands") and [Dadra and Nagar Haveli and Daman and Diu](/wiki/Dadra_and_Nagar_Haveli_and_Daman_and_Diu "Dadra and Nagar Haveli and Daman and Diu").

Although there is no specification of a national language in the constitution, it is a widely held belief that Hindi is the national language of India. This is often a source of friction and contentious debate.[[75]](#cite_note-76)[[76]](#cite_note-77)[[77]](#cite_note-78) In 2010, the [Gujarat High Court](/wiki/Gujarat_High_Court "Gujarat High Court") clarified that Hindi is not the national language of India because the constitution does not mention it as such.[[78]](#cite_note-National_TOI-79)[[79]](#cite_note-National_PTI-80)

### Fiji

Hindi is an official language in Fiji as per the 1997, 2012 and 2013 constitution's of Fiji. Two dialects of Hindi are spoken in Fiji, Standard Hindi and [Fiji Hindi](/wiki/Fiji_Hindi "Fiji Hindi"). The latter dialect traces its origins to [Awadhi](/wiki/Awadhi_language "Awadhi language"), an eastern Hindi dialect. However, Standard Hindi is the official variant of Hindi recognized by the constitution and used in all official purposes, education, media and businesses due to Fiji Hindi's lack of standardisation.[[80]](#cite_note-ethnologue.com-81)[[81]](#cite_note-82)[[82]](#cite_note-83)[[83]](#cite_note-84) Hindi is spoken by 380,000 people in Fiji.[[80]](#cite_note-ethnologue.com-81)

### Nepal

Hindi is spoken as a first language by about 77,569 people in [Nepal](/wiki/Nepal "Nepal") according to the [2011 Nepal census](/wiki/2011_Nepal_census "2011 Nepal census"), and further by 1,225,950 people as a second language.[[84]](#cite_note-85) A Hindi proponent, Indian-born [Paramananda Jha](/wiki/Paramananda_Jha "Paramananda Jha"), was elected vice-president of Nepal. He took his oath of office in Hindi in July 2008. This created protests in the streets for 5 days; students burnt his effigies, and there was a general strike in 22 districts. Nepal Supreme Court ruled in 2009 that his oath in Hindi was invalid and he was kept "inactive" as vice-president. An "angry" Jha said, "I cannot be compelled to take the oath now in Nepali. I might rather take it in English."[[85]](#cite_note-86)

### South Africa

Hindi is a protected language in [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa"). According to the [Constitution of South Africa](/wiki/Constitution_of_South_Africa "Constitution of South Africa"), the [Pan South African Language Board](/wiki/Pan_South_African_Language_Board "Pan South African Language Board") must promote and ensure respect for Hindi along with other languages.[[5]](#cite_note-auto1-6) According to a doctoral dissertation by Rajend Mesthrie in 1985, although Hindi and other Indian languages have existed in South Africa for the last 125 years, there are no academic studies of any of them – of their use in South Africa, their evolution and current decline.[[86]](#cite_note-87)

### United Arab Emirates

Hindi was adopted as the third official court language in the [Emirate of Abu Dhabi](/wiki/Emirate_of_Abu_Dhabi "Emirate of Abu Dhabi").[[6]](#cite_note-thehindu.com-7) As a result of this status, the Indian workforce in UAE can file their complaints to the labour courts in the country in their own mother-tongue.[[87]](#cite_note-88)

## Geographical distribution

Main article: [Hindustani-speaking world](/wiki/Hindustani-speaking_world "Hindustani-speaking world")

Hindi is the [lingua franca](/wiki/Lingua_franca "Lingua franca") of [northern India](/wiki/Northern_India "Northern India") (which contains the [Hindi Belt](/wiki/Hindi_Belt "Hindi Belt")), as well as an official language of the Government of India, along with English.[[67]](#cite_note-NIC2008-68)

In [Northeast India](/wiki/Northeast_India "Northeast India") a pidgin known as [Haflong Hindi](/wiki/Haflong_Hindi "Haflong Hindi") has developed as a *lingua franca* for the people living in [Haflong](/wiki/Haflong "Haflong"), [Assam](/wiki/Assam "Assam") who speak other languages natively.[[88]](#cite_note-89) In [Arunachal Pradesh](/wiki/Arunachal_Pradesh "Arunachal Pradesh"), Hindi emerged as a lingua franca among locals who speak over 50 dialects natively.[[89]](#cite_note-90)

Hindi is quite easy to understand for many [Pakistanis](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan"), who speak Urdu, which, like Hindi, is a standard register of the Hindustani language; additionally, Indian media are widely viewed in Pakistan.[[90]](#cite_note-Gandapur2012-91)

A sizeable population in [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan"), especially in [Kabul](/wiki/Kabul "Kabul"), can also speak and understand Hindi-Urdu due to the popularity and influence of [Bollywood](/wiki/Bollywood "Bollywood") films, songs and actors in the region.[[91]](#cite_note-Hakala2012-92)[[92]](#cite_note-Krishnamurthy2013-93)

Hindi is also spoken by a large population of [Madheshis](/wiki/Madheshi "Madheshi") (people having roots in north-India but having migrated to Nepal over hundreds of years) of [Nepal](/wiki/Nepal "Nepal"). Apart from this, Hindi is spoken by the large [Indian diaspora](/wiki/Indian_diaspora "Indian diaspora") which hails from, or has its origin from the "Hindi Belt" of India. A substantially large North Indian diaspora lives in countries like the [United States of America](/wiki/United_States_of_America "United States of America"), the United Kingdom, the [United Arab Emirates](/wiki/United_Arab_Emirates "United Arab Emirates"), Trinidad and Tobago, Guyana, Suriname, South Africa, Fiji and [Mauritius](/wiki/Mauritius "Mauritius"), where it is natively spoken at home and among their own Hindustani-speaking communities.

Outside India, Hindi speakers are 8 million in [Nepal](/wiki/Nepal "Nepal"); 863,077 in the [United States of America](/wiki/United_States "United States");[[93]](#cite_note-94)[[94]](#cite_note-95) 450,170 in Mauritius; 380,000 in Fiji;[[80]](#cite_note-ethnologue.com-81) 250,292 in South Africa; 150,000 in Suriname;[[95]](#cite_note-bookcitation-96) 100,000 in [Uganda](/wiki/Uganda "Uganda"); 45,800 in the United Kingdom;[[96]](#cite_note-97) 20,000 in [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand"); 20,000 in [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany"); 26,000 in Trinidad and Tobago;[[95]](#cite_note-bookcitation-96) 3,000 in [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore").

## Comparison with Standard Urdu

Main articles: [Hindi–Urdu controversy](/wiki/Hindi%E2%80%93Urdu_controversy "Hindi–Urdu controversy"), [Hindustani phonology](/wiki/Hindustani_phonology "Hindustani phonology"), and [Hindustani grammar](/wiki/Hindustani_grammar "Hindustani grammar")

[Linguistically](/wiki/Linguistics "Linguistics"), Hindi and Urdu are two registers of the same language and are mutually intelligible.[[97]](#cite_note-Daniyal2016-98) Both Hindi and Urdu share a core vocabulary of native Prakrit and Sanskrit-derived words.[[15]](#cite_note-GubeGao2019-16)[[98]](#cite_note-Kuiper2010-99)[[16]](#cite_note-ChatterjiSiṃhaPadikkal1997-17) However, Hindi is written in the Devanagari script and contains more direct *tatsama* [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit")-derived words than Urdu, whereas Urdu is written in the [Perso-Arabic](/wiki/Perso-Arabic "Perso-Arabic") script and uses more Arabic and Persian loanwords compared to Hindi.[[56]](#cite_note-JainCardona2007-57) Because of this, as well as the fact that the two registers share an identical grammar,[[99]](#cite_note-PeterDass2019-100)[[15]](#cite_note-GubeGao2019-16)[[98]](#cite_note-Kuiper2010-99) a consensus of linguists consider them to be two standardised forms of the same language, [Hindustani](/wiki/Hindustani_language "Hindustani language") or Hindi-Urdu.[[97]](#cite_note-Daniyal2016-98)[[99]](#cite_note-PeterDass2019-100)[[15]](#cite_note-GubeGao2019-16)[[100]](#cite_note-Basu2017-101) Hindi is the most commonly used scheduled language in India and is [one of the two official languages of the union](/wiki/Official_languages_of_india "Official languages of india"),[[101]](#cite_note-102) the other being English. Urdu is the [national language](/wiki/Languages_of_Pakistan#Major_regional_languages "Languages of Pakistan") and *lingua franca* of [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan") and is [one of 22 scheduled languages of India](/wiki/Scheduled_languages_of_India "Scheduled languages of India"), also having official status in [Uttar Pradesh](/wiki/Uttar_Pradesh "Uttar Pradesh"), [Jammu and Kashmir](/wiki/Jammu_and_Kashmir_(union_territory) "Jammu and Kashmir (union territory)"), [Delhi](/wiki/Delhi "Delhi"), [Telangana](/wiki/Telangana "Telangana"),[[102]](#cite_note-103) [Andhra Pradesh](/wiki/Andhra_Pradesh "Andhra Pradesh")[[103]](#cite_note-104) and [Bihar](/wiki/Bihar "Bihar").[[104]](#cite_note-105)

## Script

Main article: [Devanagari](/wiki/Devanagari "Devanagari")

Hindi is written in the Devanagari script, an [abugida](/wiki/Abugida "Abugida"). Devanagari consists of 11 [vowels](/wiki/Devanagari_script#Vowels "Devanagari script") and 33 [consonants](/wiki/Devanagari_script#Consonants "Devanagari script") and is written from left to right. Unlike Sanskrit, Devanagari is not entirely phonetic for Hindi, especially failing to mark [schwa deletion](/wiki/Schwa_deletion_in_Indo-Aryan_languages "Schwa deletion in Indo-Aryan languages") in spoken Standard Hindi.[[105]](#cite_note-106)

### Romanisation

Main article: [Devanagari transliteration](/wiki/Devanagari_transliteration "Devanagari transliteration")

The [Government of India](/wiki/Government_of_India "Government of India") uses [Hunterian transliteration](/wiki/Hunterian_transliteration "Hunterian transliteration") as its official system of writing Hindi in the Latin script. Various other systems also exist, such as [IAST](/wiki/IAST "IAST"), [ITRANS](/wiki/ITRANS "ITRANS") and [ISO 15919](/wiki/ISO_15919 "ISO 15919").

[Romanised Hindi](/wiki/Romanised_Hindi "Romanised Hindi"), also called [Hinglish](/wiki/Hinglish "Hinglish"), is the dominant form of Hindi online. In an analysis of [YouTube](/wiki/YouTube "YouTube") comments, Palakodety et al., identified that 52% of comments were in Romanised Hindi, 46% in English, and 1% in [Devanagari](/wiki/Devanagari "Devanagari") Hindi.[[106]](#cite_note-Palakodety-107)

## Vocabulary

Further information: [Hindustani etymology](/wiki/Hindustani_etymology "Hindustani etymology") and [List of Sanskrit and Persian roots in Hindi](/wiki/List_of_Sanskrit_and_Persian_roots_in_Hindi "List of Sanskrit and Persian roots in Hindi")

Traditionally, Hindi words are divided into five principal categories according to their etymology:

* **[Tatsam](/wiki/Tatsama "Tatsama")** (तत्सम transl. "same as that") words: These are words which are spelled the same in Hindi as in Sanskrit (except for the absence of final case inflections).[[116]](#cite_note-sirysq-117) They include words inherited from Sanskrit via [Prakrit](/wiki/Prakrit "Prakrit") which have survived without modification (e.g. Hindi नाम *nām* / Sanskrit नाम *nāma*, "name",[[117]](#cite_note-118) as well as forms borrowed directly from Sanskrit in more modern times (e.g. प्रार्थना *prārthanā*, "prayer").[[118]](#cite_note-119) Pronunciation, however, conforms to Hindi norms and may differ from that of classical Sanskrit. Amongst nouns, the *tatsam* word could be the Sanskrit non-inflected word-stem, or it could be the nominative singular form in the Sanskrit nominal declension.
* **Ardhatatsam** (अर्धतत्सम transl. "semi-tatsama") words: Such words are typically earlier loanwords from Sanskrit which have undergone sound changes subsequent to being borrowed. (e.g. Hindi सूरज *sūraj* from Sanskrit सूर्य *sūrya*)
* **[Tadbhav](/wiki/Tadbhava "Tadbhava")** (तद्भव transl. "born of that") words: These are native Hindi words derived from Sanskrit after undergoing phonological rules (e.g. Sanskrit कर्म *karma*, "deed" becomes [Shauraseni Prakrit](/wiki/Shauraseni_Prakrit "Shauraseni Prakrit") कम्म *kamma*, and eventually Hindi काम *kām*, "work") and are spelled differently from Sanskrit.[[116]](#cite_note-sirysq-117)
* **Deshaj** (देशज transl. "of the country") words: These are words that were not borrowings from non-indigenous languages but do not derive from attested Indo-Aryan words either. Belonging to this category are [onomatopoetic](/wiki/Onomatopoetic "Onomatopoetic") words or ones borrowed from local non-[Indo-Aryan languages](/wiki/Indo-Aryan_languages "Indo-Aryan languages").
* **Videshī** (विदेशी transl. "foreign") words: These include all [loanwords](/wiki/Loanwords "Loanwords") from non-indigenous languages. The most frequent source languages in this category are [Persian](/wiki/Persian_language "Persian language"), [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic "Arabic"), [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") and [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language"). Examples are क़िला *qila* "fort" from Persian, कमेटी *kameṭī* from English *committee*.

### Prakrit

Hindi has naturally inherited a large portion of its vocabulary from [Shauraseni Prakrit](/wiki/Shauraseni_Prakrit "Shauraseni Prakrit"), in the form of *tadbhava* words.[[16]](#cite_note-ChatterjiSiṃhaPadikkal1997-17) This process usually involves [compensatory lengthening](/wiki/Compensatory_lengthening "Compensatory lengthening") of vowels preceding consonant clusters in Prakrit, e.g. Sanskrit *tīkṣṇa* > Prakrit *tikkha* > Hindi *tīkhā*.

### Sanskrit

Much of Standard Hindi's vocabulary is borrowed from Sanskrit as *tatsam* borrowings, especially in technical and academic fields. The formal Hindi standard, from which much of the Persian, Arabic and English vocabulary has been replaced by [neologisms](/wiki/Neologism "Neologism") compounding *tatsam* words, is called *Śuddh Hindi* (pure Hindi), and is viewed as a more prestigious dialect over other more colloquial forms of Hindi.

Excessive use of *tatsam* words sometimes creates problems for native speakers. They may have Sanskrit consonant clusters which do not exist in Hindustani, causing difficulties in pronunciation.[[119]](#cite_note-120)

As a part of the process of [Sanskritisation](/wiki/Sanskritisation "Sanskritisation"), new words are coined using Sanskrit components to be used as replacements for supposedly foreign vocabulary. Usually these neologisms are [calques](/wiki/Calque "Calque") of English words already adopted into spoken Hindi. Some terms such as *dūrbhāṣ* "telephone", literally "far-speech" and *dūrdarśan* "television", literally "far-sight" have even gained some currency in formal Hindi in the place of the English borrowings *(ṭeli)fon* and *ṭīvī*.[[120]](#cite_note-121)

### Persian

Hindi also features significant [Persian](/wiki/Persian_language "Persian language") influence, standardised from spoken [Hindustani](/wiki/Hindustani_language "Hindustani language").[[56]](#cite_note-JainCardona2007-57)[[38]](#cite_note-kachru-39)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] Early borrowings, beginning in the mid-12th century, were specific to [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") (e.g. *Muhammad*, *Islām*) and so Persian was simply an intermediary for Arabic. Later, under the [Delhi Sultanate](/wiki/Delhi_Sultanate "Delhi Sultanate") and [Mughal Empire](/wiki/Mughal_Empire "Mughal Empire"), Persian became the primary administrative language in the Hindi heartland. Persian borrowings reached a heyday in the 17th century, pervading all aspects of life. Even grammatical constructs, namely the [izafat](/wiki/Izafat "Izafat"), were assimilated into Hindi.[[121]](#cite_note-122)

The status of Persian language then and thus its influence, is also visible in Hindi [proverbs](/wiki/Proverb "Proverb"):

|  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- |
| हाथ कंगन को आरसी क्या,  पढ़े लिखे को फ़ारसी क्या। | *Hāth kaṅgan ko ārsī kyā,  Paṛhe likhe ko Fārsī kyā.* | What is mirror to a hand with bangles,  What is Persian to a literate. |

The emergence of Modern Standard Hindi in the 19th century went along with the Sanskritisation of its vocabulary,[[122]](#cite_note-123) leading to a marginalisation of Persian vocabulary in Hindi, which continued after [Partition](/wiki/Partition_of_India "Partition of India") when the Indian government co-opted the policy of Sanskritisation. However, many Persian words (e.g. *bas* "enough", *khud* "self") have remained entrenched in Standard Hindi, and a larger amount are still used in [Urdu poetry](/wiki/Urdu_poetry "Urdu poetry") written in the Devanagari script. Many words borrowed from Persian in turn were loanwords from Arabic (e.g. *muśkil* "difficult", *havā* "air", *x(a)yāl* "thought", *kitāb* "book").

## Media

### Literature

Main article: [Hindi literature](/wiki/Hindi_literature "Hindi literature")

Hindi literature is broadly divided into four prominent forms or styles, being *[Bhakti](/wiki/Bhakti "Bhakti")* (devotional – [Kabir](/wiki/Kabirdas "Kabirdas"), [Raskhan](/wiki/Raskhan "Raskhan")); *Śṛṇgār* (beauty – [Keshav](/wiki/Keshav "Keshav"), [Bihari](/wiki/Bihari_(poet) "Bihari (poet)")); *Vīgāthā* (epic); and *Ādhunik* (modern).

Medieval Hindi literature is marked by the influence of [Bhakti movement](/wiki/Bhakti_movement "Bhakti movement") and the composition of long, epic poems. It was primarily written in other [varieties of Hindi](/wiki/Hindi_languages "Hindi languages"), particularly [Avadhi](/wiki/Avadhi "Avadhi") and [Braj Bhasha](/wiki/Braj_Bhasha "Braj Bhasha"), but to a degree also in [Dehlavi](/wiki/Dehlavi_dialect "Dehlavi dialect"), the basis for Standard Hindi. During the [British Raj](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj"), Hindustani became the prestige dialect.

[*Chandrakanta*](/wiki/Chandrakanta_(novel) "Chandrakanta (novel)"), written by [Devaki Nandan Khatri](/wiki/Devaki_Nandan_Khatri "Devaki Nandan Khatri") in 1888, is considered the first authentic work of prose in modern Hindi.[[125]](#cite_note-128) The person who brought realism in Hindi prose literature was [Munshi Premchand](/wiki/Munshi_Premchand "Munshi Premchand"), who is considered the most revered figure in the world of Hindi fiction and progressive movement. Literary, or *Sāhityik*, Hindi was popularised by the writings of [Swami Dayananda Saraswati](/wiki/Swami_Dayananda_Saraswati "Swami Dayananda Saraswati"), [Bhartendu Harishchandra](/wiki/Bhartendu_Harishchandra "Bhartendu Harishchandra") and others. The rising numbers of newspapers and magazines made Hindustani popular with educated people.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The *Dvivedī Yug* ("Age of Dwivedi") in Hindi literature lasted from 1900 to 1918. It is named after [Mahavir Prasad Dwivedi](/wiki/Mahavir_Prasad_Dwivedi "Mahavir Prasad Dwivedi"), who played a major role in establishing Standard Hindi in poetry and broadening the acceptable subjects of Hindi poetry from the traditional ones of religion and romantic love.

In the 20th century, Hindi literature saw a romantic upsurge. This is known as *[Chāyāvād](/wiki/Chhayavaad "Chhayavaad")* (*shadow-ism*) and the literary figures belonging to this school are known as *Chāyāvādī*. [Jaishankar Prasad](/wiki/Jaishankar_Prasad "Jaishankar Prasad"), [Suryakant Tripathi 'Nirala'](/wiki/Suryakant_Tripathi_%27Nirala%27 "Suryakant Tripathi 'Nirala'"), [Mahadevi Varma](/wiki/Mahadevi_Varma "Mahadevi Varma") and [Sumitranandan Pant](/wiki/Sumitranandan_Pant "Sumitranandan Pant"), are the four major *Chāyāvādī* poets.

*Uttar Ādhunik* is the post-modernist period of Hindi literature, marked by a questioning of early trends that copied the West as well as the excessive ornamentation of the *Chāyāvādī* movement, and by a return to simple language and natural themes.

### Internet

Hindi literature, [music](/wiki/Music "Music"), and [film](/wiki/Bollywood "Bollywood") have all been disseminated via the internet. In 2015, Google reported a 94% increase in Hindi-content consumption year-on-year, adding that 21% of users in India prefer content in Hindi.[[126]](#cite_note-129) Many Hindi [newspapers](/wiki/Newspaper "Newspaper") also offer digital editions.

"The primary sources of non-IA loans into MSH are Arabic, Persian, Portuguese, Turkic and English. Conversational registers of Hindi/Urdu (not to mentioned formal registers of Urdu) employ large numbers of Persian and Arabic loanwords, although in Sanskritized registers many of these words are replaced by *tatsama* forms from Sanskrit. The Persian and Arabic lexical elements in Hindi result from the effects of centuries of Islamic administrative rule over much of north India in the centuries before the establishment of British rule in India. Although it is conventional to differentiate among Persian and Arabic loan elements into Hindi/Urdu, in practice it is often difficult to separate these strands from one another. The Arabic (and also Turkic) lexemes borrowed into Hindi frequently were mediated through Persian, as a result of which a thorough intertwining of Persian and Arabic elements took place, as manifest by such phenomena as hybrid compounds and compound words. Moreover, although the dominant trajectory of lexical borrowing was from Arabic into Persian, and thence into Hindi/Urdu, examples can be found of words that in origin are actually Persian loanwords into both Arabic and Hindi/Urdu."
